title: CJNG Co-Founder 'El 85' Pleads Guilty to US Drug Trafficking — Sentencing July 31
detail: Erick Valencia-Salazar, 49, alias 'El 85,' co-founder of the Jalisco New Generation Cartel (CJNG) alongside El Mencho, pleaded guilty in US federal court to one count of conspiring to distribute cocaine for importation into the United States. He faces a mandatory minimum of 10 years and up to life in prison; sentencing is set for July 31, 2026 before Chief Judge James Boasberg. Valencia-Salazar later broke from CJNG to establish La Nueva Plaza, a rival Jalisco-based group, but his origins at CJNG's founding make his plea a significant legal milestone — coming just 44 days after El Mencho's killing by Mexican forces in Tapalpa. The guilty plea adds to mounting legal pressure on the organization's succession leadership under O3 and represents the second major CJNG accountability action in two months after El Mencho's elimination.

title: El Mayo Zambada Sentencing Set for April 13 — Prosecution Files Final Rebuttal
detail: US federal prosecutors filed their sentencing rebuttal memo in the case of Ismael 'El Mayo' Zambada García ahead of the April 13, 2026 sentencing hearing in Brooklyn federal court. El Mayo, 77, pleaded guilty to leading a continuing criminal enterprise (RICO) and faces mandatory life imprisonment plus a $15 billion forfeiture judgment. The sentencing was originally scheduled for January 13 but was delayed at defense request. The proceeding marks the formal legal conclusion of one of the most consequential cartel captures in history — El Mayo's arrest on July 25, 2024 fractured the Sinaloa Cartel and triggered the ongoing civil war between Los Chapitos and Los Mayos that has devastated Sinaloa and displaced tens of thousands. El Mayo's lawyers are expected to argue circumstances of his alleged kidnapping by Joaquín Guzmán López in mitigation.

title: Transport Workers Blockades Across 20+ States Pressure Sheinbaum on Cartel Insecurity
detail: One day after transport workers organized highway blockades across more than 20 of Mexico's 32 states, the demonstrations continue to reverberate. ANTAC (National Association of Transport Drivers) and FNRCM led the protest against rampant cartel violence targeting freight operations: approximately 40 trucks are hijacked daily in Mexico, 80% with violence. Cargo theft, kidnappings of drivers, extortion payments at cartel checkpoints, and rising fuel costs have made freight transport untenable across large sections of the country. The blockades represent a significant grassroots pressure on President Sheinbaum to produce tangible security outcomes — particularly as the World Cup (June 11) security preparations consume federal resources and attention. Sheinbaum's security secretary pledged additional National Guard deployments along key commercial corridors.
